questions.prompt: What does defining an enum like `enum IpAddrKind { V4, V6 }` express in Rust's type system?

options.A: A value of type `IpAddrKind` is exactly one of a fixed set of possible variants
options.B: A value of type `IpAddrKind` contains both a `V4` and a `V6` field at the same time
options.C: `V4` and `V6` are two independent types with no relationship to each other
options.D: `IpAddrKind` is an alias for an integer that can hold any value

questions.explanation: An enum says a value is one of a possible set of variants — it can be `V4` or `V6`, but never both at once. Both variants are values of the same type, `IpAddrKind`.

questions.prompt: Given `enum IpAddrKind { V4, V6 }`, how do you create an instance of the `V4` variant?

options.A: `let four = V4;` — variants are global names
options.B: `let four = IpAddrKind::V4;` — variants are namespaced under the enum with `::`
options.C: `let four = IpAddrKind.V4;` — variants are accessed with a dot
options.D: `let four = new IpAddrKind::V4();` — variants must be constructed with `new`

questions.explanation: Enum variants are namespaced under the enum's identifier, so you use the double colon: `IpAddrKind::V4`. (Only `Option`'s `Some`/`None` are usable without a prefix, because the prelude imports them.)

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements about enum variants in Rust are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Different variants of the same enum can hold different types and amounts of data
options.B: Every variant of an enum must hold the same type of data
options.C: A variant can have struct-like named fields, as `Move { x: i32, y: i32 }` does
options.D: Variants that hold data must first be defined as separate structs
options.E: You can define methods on an enum using an `impl` block, just like on a struct

questions.explanation: Variants may carry no data (`Quit`), named fields (`Move`), or tuple-like data of any types (`Write`, `ChangeColor`), and each variant can differ. Enums also support `impl` blocks for methods; no separate struct definitions are required.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.A: It prints `write: hello`
options.B: It prints `quit`
options.C: It fails to compile because enums cannot have `impl` blocks
options.D: It fails to compile because `describe` does not handle the `Write` case

questions.explanation: Enums can have methods defined in `impl` blocks just like structs. `m` is the `Write` variant, so the match binds `text` to the inner `String` and the method returns `write: hello`.

questions.prompt: Why does Rust use `Option<T>` instead of a null value like many other languages?

options.A: Because `Option<T>` and `T` are different types, so the compiler forces you to handle the absent case before using the value
options.B: Because `Option<T>` is faster at runtime than checking for null
options.C: Because Rust has null, but only inside `unsafe` blocks, and `Option<T>` wraps that mechanism
options.D: Because `Option<T>` automatically converts to `T` whenever a value is present

questions.explanation: Rust has no null. Since `Option<T>` is a distinct type from `T`, the compiler will not let you use an optional value as if it were definitely present — you must explicitly handle both `Some` and `None`, eliminating the classic null-dereference bug.

questions.prompt: What is the result of running this program?

options.A: It prints `10` — `Some(5)` is unwrapped automatically
options.B: It compiles but panics at runtime when adding the `Option`
options.C: It fails to compile: there is no implementation for `i8 + Option<i8>`
options.D: It prints `5` — `None`-like values are treated as zero

questions.explanation: `Option<i8>` and `i8` are different types, so `x + y` is rejected at compile time (error E0277: cannot add `Option<i8>` to `i8`). You must convert the `Option<i8>` to an `i8` — for example with `match` — before adding.

questions.prompt: Why does this program fail to compile?

options.A: `None` cannot be assigned to a variable; it can only appear in `match` arms
options.B: The compiler cannot infer the `T` in `Option<T>` from `None` alone, so a type annotation like `Option<i32>` is required
options.C: `None` is not in scope unless you write `use std::option::Option::None;`
options.D: Variables holding `None` must be declared `mut` so they can later hold `Some`

questions.explanation: With `Some(5)` the compiler infers `Option<i32>`, but `None` carries no value to infer `T` from, so you must annotate, e.g. `let absent_number: Option<i32> = None;`. `None` itself is in scope via the prelude.

questions.prompt: A colleague says: "`Some(5)` has type `i32`, since it just wraps the number 5." What is the correct picture?

options.A: They are right — `Some(5)` and `5` are interchangeable
options.B: `Some(5)` has type `Option<i32>`, a different type from `i32`, so you must extract the inner value before using it as an `i32`
options.C: `Some(5)` has type `Some<i32>`, which coerces to `i32` on demand
options.D: `Some(5)` has no type until it is matched

questions.explanation: `Some` is a variant of `enum Option<T> { None, Some(T) }`, so `Some(5)` is an `Option<i32>`. The deliberate separation between `Option<T>` and `T` is what forces code to handle the possibly-absent case.

questions.prompt: In a `match` expression, which arm's code runs, and what does the whole `match` evaluate to?

options.A: Every arm whose pattern matches runs, and the `match` evaluates to the last one
options.B: The first arm whose pattern matches runs, and the `match` evaluates to that arm's expression
options.C: The most specific matching arm runs regardless of order, and the `match` evaluates to `()`
options.D: Arms are tried in random order, so a catch-all must always be present

questions.explanation: Patterns are compared in order, like coins falling through a sorting machine: the first pattern the value fits wins. The resulting value of the chosen arm's expression becomes the value of the entire `match` expression.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.A: Only `25`
options.B: `State quarter from Alaska!` followed by `25`
options.C: It fails to compile because `state` is not declared anywhere
options.D: `State quarter from UsState!` followed by `25`

questions.explanation: The pattern `Coin::Quarter(state)` binds the variable `state` to the data inside the variant, here `UsState::Alaska`. The arm prints the debug form of the state and then evaluates to `25`, which `main` prints.

questions.prompt: What is wrong with this function?

options.A: Nothing — if `x` is `None`, the match simply returns `None` by default
options.B: It compiles but panics at runtime when called with `None`
options.C: It fails to compile: matches are exhaustive, and the `None` case is not covered
options.D: It fails to compile because `i` shadows the function parameter

questions.explanation: Rust `match` arms must cover every possible value; here the compiler rejects the code with a `non-exhaustive patterns: None not covered` error. Adding a `None => None` arm (or a catch-all) fixes it — a bug caught at compile time, not runtime.

questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.A: `Some(6)` then `None`
options.B: `6` then `0`
options.C: `Some(6)` then a panic on the second call
options.D: `Some(5)` then `None` — `i + 1` modifies a copy that is discarded

questions.explanation: For `Some(5)` the pattern `Some(i)` binds `i` to `5` and the arm produces `Some(6)`; for `None` the first arm matches and returns `None`. `{:?}` prints the `Option` values in debug form.

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements about catch-all patterns in `match` are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: Using `_` as the final arm makes the match non-exhaustive
options.B: A catch-all arm should be placed last, because patterns are evaluated in order
options.C: A named pattern like `other` matches any remaining value and binds it so the arm can use it
options.D: The `_` pattern matches any remaining value but does not bind it
options.E: Putting an arm after a catch-all is a hard compile error

questions.explanation: `other` and `_` both satisfy exhaustiveness; the difference is that `other` binds the value while `_` tells Rust you won't use it. Order matters because arms are tried top to bottom — an arm after a catch-all is unreachable, which produces a compiler warning, not an error.

questions.prompt: What happens when you compile this program?

options.A: It prints `got: hello` and then `Some("hello")`
options.B: It fails to compile: matching on `opt` by value moves the `String` into `s`, so `opt` is partially moved and cannot be used afterward
options.C: It compiles but panics at runtime when printing the moved value
options.D: It fails to compile because `match` never takes ownership of the value it inspects

questions.explanation: `String` is not `Copy`, so the pattern `Some(s)` moves the inner `String` out of `opt`, leaving it partially moved; the later use of `opt` is a compile error (E0382). Matching on a reference instead — `match &opt` — borrows the inner value and leaves `opt` usable.

questions.prompt: In the arm `_ => ()`, a beginner claims `()` is "an empty function call that does nothing". What does `()` actually mean here?

options.A: `()` is the unit value; the arm matches every other value and explicitly does nothing
options.B: `()` calls the default handler registered for the enum
options.C: `()` is a compile error — an arm must contain at least one statement
options.D: `()` returns `None`, since a `match` must produce an `Option`

questions.explanation: `()` is the unit value, the value of the empty tuple type. Using it as an arm's expression says: this pattern matches anything else, and we run no code for it.

questions.prompt: Why does this program fail to compile?

options.A: The `None` arm is unreachable because `x` is known to be `Some`
options.B: A `match` used as an expression requires all arms to produce the same type, but one arm yields `i32` and the other `&str`
options.C: String literals are not allowed inside `match` arms
options.D: `result` must be annotated with a type before the `match` can compile

questions.explanation: Because the whole `match` is an expression assigned to `result`, every arm must evaluate to a single common type. Here the arms produce `i32` and `&str`, so the compiler reports mismatched types (E0308).

questions.section: 6.3 Concise Control Flow with if let and let else
questions.prompt: Which of the following statements about `if let` are true? (Select all that apply.)

options.A: `if let` is syntactic sugar for a `match` that runs code for one pattern and ignores all other values
options.B: If the pattern does not match, the program panics at runtime
options.C: Using `if let` means giving up the exhaustiveness checking that `match` enforces
options.D: An `else` block attached to `if let` behaves like the `_` catch-all arm of the equivalent `match`
options.E: `if let` must always be followed by an `else` block

questions.explanation: `if let` handles exactly one pattern and simply skips the block (or runs the optional `else`) when the value doesn't match — no panic, but also no compiler check that every case is handled. The `else` corresponds to the `_ =>` arm of the equivalent `match`.

questions.section: 6.3 Concise Control Flow with if let and let else
questions.prompt: What does this program print?

options.A: `State quarter from Alaska!` then `count = 2`
options.B: `State quarter from Alaska!` then `count = 3`
options.C: `count = 2` only — `if let` cannot bind `state`
options.D: It fails to compile because `if let` with `else` is not valid syntax

questions.explanation: The quarter matches the `if let` pattern, binding `state` and printing the message; the two pennies fall into the `else` branch, incrementing `count` to 2. `if let ... else` is exactly like a two-arm `match` with a `_` arm.

questions.section: 6.3 Concise Control Flow with if let and let else
questions.prompt: What is wrong with this use of `let...else`?

options.A: Nothing — after printing, `n` defaults to `0` and the function returns `0`
options.B: It fails to compile: the `else` block of `let...else` must diverge (e.g. `return` or `panic!`), because `n` would otherwise be unbound
options.C: It fails to compile because `let...else` only works with the `Result` type
options.D: It compiles but panics at runtime whenever `x` is `None`

questions.explanation: With `let PATTERN = EXPR else { ... }`, the `else` block runs when the pattern doesn't match, and it must exit the enclosing scope (return, panic, break, etc.) since no binding for `n` exists on that path. Adding `return 0;` after the `println!` would fix it.

questions.section: 6.3 Concise Control Flow with if let and let else
questions.prompt: What is the main purpose of `let...else`, as in the `describe_state_quarter` example?

options.A: To retry the pattern match repeatedly until it succeeds
options.B: To extract a binding into the surrounding scope on a match, while handling the non-matching case with an early exit — keeping the happy path unindented
options.C: To make the compiler treat the pattern as exhaustive without listing other variants
options.D: To shadow `coin` with a new variable of a different type

questions.explanation: `let...else` puts the successful binding (`state`) directly in the outer scope and forces the non-matching case into a diverging `else` (here `return None;`). This keeps the main logic on the "happy path" without nesting it inside a `match` or `if let` block.
